title: Literal Types
description: Lock down exact values as types.

slides.title: Beyond 'Just a String' 🎯
slides.content:
Using `string` is good, but what if a function accepts the status of an order, and the status can ONLY be 'pending', 'shipped', or 'delivered'?

If you use `string`, someone could pass 'dancing', and the code compiles fine.
Literal Types fix this!

slides.title: Literal Types
slides.content: A literal type allows you to specify the EXACT, literal string (or number) that is allowed.

quizData.question: Can you create literal types using exact numbers instead of strings?

options.text: No, literal types are strictly for strings.
Yes, but they act like standard `number` types internally.

quizData.explanation: Absolutely. You can constrain a variable to exact literal numbers, too!

slides.title: Massive Intellisense Win ✨
slides.content:
When dealing with literal types, your code editor (like VS Code) becomes incredibly smart.

When you call a function requiring a literal union, hitting 'Space' will instantly pop up a menu offering only those exact options to autocomplete!

slides.title: The 'const' Secret
slides.content:
If you use `let name = 'Alice';`, TS infers it as `string`.
But if you use `const name = 'Alice';`, TS automatically infers the literal type `"Alice"` because `const` values can never change!
